categories.name: File Handling — Text Files
categories.description: Text file operations are asked every year for 3-5 marks. Master read(), readline(), readlines() and the with statement.

programs.title: Count vowels, consonants, uppercase and lowercase in a file
programs.description: Read a text file and count the number of vowels, consonants, uppercase letters and lowercase letters.
programs.output:
Vowels: 15
Consonants: 25
Uppercase: 4
Lowercase: 36
programs.tips: Always close the file after use. Use ch.isalpha() before checking vowel/consonant to skip spaces and punctuation. The examiner checks for this.

programs.title: Count digits, spaces and special characters in a file
programs.description: Read a text file and count digits, blank spaces and other (special) characters.
programs.output:
Digits: 5
Spaces: 12
Special characters: 3
programs.tips: Use elif for mutually exclusive conditions. isspace() covers spaces, tabs and newlines. Do not count alphabets as special characters.

programs.title: Count words and lines in a file
programs.description: Read a text file and display the total number of words and lines.
programs.code:
def count_words_lines():
    fh = open('story.txt', 'r')
    lines = fh.readlines()
    word_count = 0
    for line in lines:
        words = line.split()
        word_count += len(words)
    fh.close()
    print('Number of words:', word_count)
    print('Number of lines:', len(lines))

count_words_lines()
programs.output:
Number of words: 42
Number of lines: 5
programs.tips: Use split() without arguments — it handles multiple spaces and tabs correctly. len(lines) gives the line count directly.

programs.title: Display lines containing a specific word
programs.description: Read a text file and display all lines that contain the word 'India'.
programs.output:
India is a great country.
I love India very much.
programs.tips: Use 'in' operator for substring search. Use end='' in print to avoid double newlines since each line already has a newline character.

programs.title: Display words starting with a specific letter
programs.description: Read a text file and display all words that start with the letter 'T' or 't'.
programs.output:
The
to
their
Technology
programs.tips: Convert both to lowercase for case-insensitive comparison. Use word[0] to access the first character. Make sure file is not empty before calling split().

programs.title: Display words starting AND ending with a vowel
programs.description: Read a text file and display all words that start with a vowel and also end with a vowel.
programs.output:
are
educate
Indie
programs.tips: Use word[-1] to access the last character. Include both uppercase and lowercase vowels in the check string, or convert to lower() before checking.

programs.title: Copy lines from one file to another
programs.description: Read a text file and copy only those lines that start with an uppercase letter to another file.
programs.output: Lines copied successfully
programs.tips: Open source in 'r' mode and target in 'w' mode. Check line[0] carefully — empty lines may cause IndexError. Always close both files.

programs.title: Count frequency of a word in file
programs.description: Read a text file and count how many times a given word occurs in it.
programs.code:
def count_word(word):
    fh = open('story.txt', 'r')
    data = fh.read()
    words = data.split()
    count = 0
    for w in words:
        if w.lower() == word.lower():
            count += 1
    fh.close()
    print('"' + word + '" occurs', count, 'times')

count_word('the')
programs.output: "the" occurs 4 times
programs.tips: Convert both words to lowercase for case-insensitive matching. You can also use words.count(word) but it is case-sensitive. The manual loop approach is preferred by examiners.

categories.name: File Handling — Binary Files
categories.description: Binary file handling with pickle module is asked every year for 3-4 marks. Always import pickle and use 'rb'/'wb'/'ab' modes.

programs.title: Write student records to binary file
programs.description: Accept student data (roll number, name, marks) and write to a binary file using pickle.
programs.code:
import pickle

def write_records():
    fh = open('students.dat', 'wb')
    n = int(input('How many records? '))
    for i in range(n):
        roll = int(input('Enter roll number: '))
        name = input('Enter name: ')
        marks = float(input('Enter marks: '))
        record = [roll, name, marks]
        pickle.dump(record, fh)
    fh.close()
    print(n, 'records written successfully')

write_records()
programs.output:
How many records? 2
Enter roll number: 1
Enter name: Aman
Enter marks: 89.5
Enter roll number: 2
Enter name: Neha
Enter marks: 92.0
2 records written successfully
programs.tips: Always write 'import pickle' as the first line. Use 'wb' mode to write. Each record is dumped separately using pickle.dump(). Store records as list or dictionary.

programs.title: Read and display all records from binary file
programs.description: Read all student records from a binary file and display them.
programs.output:
Roll: 1 Name: Aman Marks: 89.5
Roll: 2 Name: Neha Marks: 92.0
--- End of file ---
programs.tips: Use try-except with EOFError to detect end of file. This is the standard CBSE pattern — examiners expect this. Never forget the 'rb' mode for reading binary files.

programs.title: Search for a specific record in binary file
programs.description: Search for a student record by roll number in a binary file.
programs.output:
Record found!
Roll: 2
Name: Neha
Marks: 92.0
programs.tips: Use a boolean flag 'found' to track whether the record was located. Do NOT use break after finding — continue reading to close properly via EOFError, or close in a finally block.

programs.title: Update a record in binary file
programs.description: Update the marks of a student identified by roll number in a binary file.
programs.output: Record updated successfully
programs.tips: Read all records into a list first, modify the required record, then rewrite all records. This is the safest approach for binary files. Use 'wb' when rewriting to overwrite the old file.

categories.name: File Handling — CSV Files
categories.description: CSV file handling is asked frequently for 3-4 marks. Always import csv module and understand the difference between writer/reader objects.

programs.title: Write data to CSV file
programs.description: Write student records to a CSV file using csv.writer().
programs.code:
import csv

def write_csv():
    fh = open('students.csv', 'w', newline='')
    writer = csv.writer(fh)
    writer.writerow(['Roll', 'Name', 'Marks'])
    n = int(input('How many records? '))
    for i in range(n):
        roll = int(input('Enter roll number: '))
        name = input('Enter name: ')
        marks = float(input('Enter marks: '))
        writer.writerow([roll, name, marks])
    fh.close()
    print('Data written to CSV file')

write_csv()
programs.output:
How many records? 2
Enter roll number: 1
Enter name: Aman
Enter marks: 89.5
Enter roll number: 2
Enter name: Neha
Enter marks: 92.0
Data written to CSV file
programs.tips: Always pass newline='' when opening CSV file to avoid blank rows on Windows. Write the header row first using writerow(). Use 'w' mode for writing.

programs.title: Read and display data from CSV file
programs.description: Read all records from a CSV file and display them using csv.reader().
programs.code:
import csv

def read_csv():
    fh = open('students.csv', 'r')
    reader = csv.reader(fh)
    for row in reader:
        print(row)
    fh.close()

read_csv()
programs.tips: csv.reader() returns each row as a list of strings. Even numbers are read as strings. Use int() or float() if you need to do calculations. The first row is usually the header.

programs.title: Search records in CSV file
programs.description: Search for a student by name in a CSV file and display matching records.
programs.code:
import csv

def search_csv(search_name):
    fh = open('students.csv', 'r')
    reader = csv.reader(fh)
    header = next(reader)
    found = False
    for row in reader:
        if row[1].lower() == search_name.lower():
            print('Roll:', row[0])
            print('Name:', row[1])
            print('Marks:', row[2])
            found = True
    fh.close()
    if not found:
        print('Record not found')

search_csv('Neha')
programs.output:
Roll: 2
Name: Neha
Marks: 92.0
programs.tips: Use next(reader) to skip the header row before searching. All values in CSV are strings, so compare as strings or convert types. Use lower() for case-insensitive search.

categories.name: Stack Operations
categories.description: Stack implementation using Python list is asked every year for 3-4 marks. Use append() for push and pop() for pop. Always check for underflow.

programs.title: Stack — Push and Pop integers
programs.description: Implement push and pop operations on a stack of integers using a list.
programs.output:
10 pushed to stack
20 pushed to stack
30 pushed to stack
Stack (top to bottom):
30
20
10
30 popped from stack
Stack (top to bottom):
20
10
programs.tips: Always check for empty stack before pop — this is called underflow. Display from top to bottom using reverse traversal. Use append() for push, pop() for pop — never use insert() or remove().

programs.title: Stack — Push and Pop strings
programs.description: Implement a stack to store and manage a list of book titles.
programs.output:
"Python Basics" added to stack
"Data Structures" added to stack
"DBMS" added to stack
Books in stack (top to bottom):
DBMS
Data Structures
Python Basics
"DBMS" removed from stack
Books in stack (top to bottom):
Data Structures
Python Basics
programs.tips: The logic is the same regardless of data type. Examiners often change the context (books, cities, names) but the push/pop/display structure remains identical.

programs.title: Stack — Push and Pop employee tuples
programs.description: Implement a stack where each element is a tuple containing employee number and name. Push only those employees whose salary is above 50000.
programs.output:
(101, 'Aman') pushed to stack
Neha not eligible (salary <= 50000)
(103, 'Ravi') pushed to stack
Employee Stack (top to bottom):
EmpNo: 103 Name: Ravi
EmpNo: 101 Name: Aman
(103, 'Ravi') popped from stack
Employee Stack (top to bottom):
EmpNo: 101 Name: Aman
programs.tips: This is the most common board exam pattern — push with a condition. The tuple stores only selected fields, not all. Read the question carefully to identify which fields go into the tuple and what the push condition is.

programs.title: Stack — Display without deleting (peek and traverse)
programs.description: Display all elements of a stack without removing them, and also show the topmost element.
programs.output:
Top element: 50
Stack contents:
50 <-- TOP
40
30
20
10
programs.tips: Use stack[-1] to peek at the top without removing. To display, traverse from the last index to 0 using range(len(stack)-1, -1, -1). Never use pop() in display function.

categories.name: Python Functions — Output Prediction
categories.description: Output prediction questions on functions test understanding of scope, default arguments and recursion. Worth 1-3 marks.

programs.title: Function with default arguments
programs.description: Predict the output of a function that uses default parameter values.
programs.code:
def greet(name, msg='Good Morning'):
    print('Hello', name + ',', msg)

greet('Aman')
greet('Neha', 'Good Evening')
greet(msg='Good Night', name='Ravi')
programs.output:
Hello Aman, Good Morning
Hello Neha, Good Evening
Hello Ravi, Good Night
programs.tips: Default arguments are used when a value is not passed. When using keyword arguments, the order does not matter. Default parameters must come after non-default parameters in the function definition.

programs.title: Function with mutable default argument (list)
programs.description: Predict the output when a list is used as a default argument — a classic Python trap.
programs.code:
def add_item(item, lst=[]):
    lst.append(item)
    print(lst)

add_item(1)
add_item(2)
add_item(3, [10, 20])
programs.tips: Mutable default arguments (like lists) are shared across all calls. The default list is created once and reused. When a new list is passed explicitly, the default is not used for that call.

programs.title: Global vs Local variable scope
programs.description: Predict the output involving global and local variables with the same name.
programs.code:
x = 10

def func1():
    x = 20
    print('Inside func1, x =', x)

def func2():
    global x
    x = 30
    print('Inside func2, x =', x)

func1()
print('After func1, x =', x)
func2()
print('After func2, x =', x)
programs.output:
Inside func1, x = 20
After func1, x = 10
Inside func2, x = 30
After func2, x = 30
programs.tips: Without 'global' keyword, assignment creates a local variable that does not affect the global one. With 'global' keyword, the function modifies the actual global variable. This is the most common scope question in CBSE.

programs.title: Recursive function — Factorial
programs.description: Write a recursive function to find the factorial of a number.
programs.output: Factorial of 5 = 120
programs.tips: Every recursive function must have a base case (here n==0 or n==1). The recursive call must move towards the base case. Trace the calls: factorial(5) = 5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * 1 = 120.

programs.title: Recursive function — Sum of digits
programs.description: Write a recursive function to find the sum of digits of a number.
programs.output: Sum of digits of 1234 = 10
programs.tips: Use n % 10 to get the last digit and n // 10 to remove it. Base case is when n becomes 0. Trace: 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10.

programs.title: Recursive function — Fibonacci series
programs.description: Write a recursive function to return the nth Fibonacci number.
programs.tips: Fibonacci has two base cases: fib(1)=0 and fib(2)=1. Each call makes two recursive calls so it is slower for large n. CBSE usually asks for small values only.

programs.title: Recursive function — Power (x raised to n)
programs.description: Write a recursive function to calculate x raised to the power n.
programs.output: 2 raised to 5 = 32
programs.tips: Base case: any number raised to power 0 is 1. Recursive step: x^n = x * x^(n-1). This is a clean example that examiners love to ask.

categories.name: SQL Queries — Common Patterns
categories.description: SQL queries are asked for 2-5 marks in every board exam. Practice writing queries based on given table structures. Questions may ask you to write a query or predict the output.

programs.title: SELECT with WHERE, BETWEEN, IN, LIKE
programs.description: Write queries using common WHERE clause operators on an EMPLOYEE table (EmpNo, Name, Department, Salary, City).
programs.output:
-- Results depend on table data --
-- BETWEEN is inclusive of both limits
-- IN checks for membership in a set
-- % matches zero or more characters
-- _ matches exactly one character
programs.tips: BETWEEN is inclusive (includes both endpoints). LIKE patterns: % for any number of characters, _ for exactly one character. IN is shorthand for multiple OR conditions. String values must be in single quotes.

programs.title: GROUP BY with HAVING
programs.description: Write queries using GROUP BY and HAVING on the EMPLOYEE table.
programs.code:
-- Count employees in each department
SELECT Department, COUNT(*) AS Total
FROM EMPLOYEE
GROUP BY Department;

-- Display departments with more than 3 employees
SELECT Department, COUNT(*) AS Total
FROM EMPLOYEE
GROUP BY Department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 3;

-- Average salary per department, only for departments with avg > 40000
SELECT Department, AVG(Salary) AS AvgSalary
FROM EMPLOYEE
GROUP BY Department
HAVING AVG(Salary) > 40000;
programs.output:
-- GROUP BY groups rows with same column values
-- HAVING filters groups (like WHERE for groups)
-- WHERE filters rows BEFORE grouping
-- HAVING filters groups AFTER grouping
programs.tips: WHERE filters individual rows before grouping. HAVING filters groups after grouping. You cannot use aggregate functions in WHERE — use HAVING instead. Always write GROUP BY before HAVING.

programs.title: ORDER BY — Sorting results
programs.description: Write queries to sort results in ascending and descending order.
programs.output:
-- ASC = ascending (default, A-Z, 0-9)
-- DESC = descending (Z-A, 9-0)
-- Multiple columns: first sorts by first column, then by second
programs.tips: ORDER BY is always the last clause before LIMIT. ASC is the default sort order. You can sort by multiple columns. NULL values appear first in ASC order. LIMIT is used to restrict the number of rows returned.

programs.title: Aggregate Functions — COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN
programs.description: Write queries using SQL aggregate functions on the EMPLOYEE table.
programs.output:
-- COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULL
-- COUNT(column) counts non-NULL values only
-- SUM, AVG work only on numeric columns
-- MAX, MIN work on all data types
programs.tips: COUNT(*) counts all rows. COUNT(column_name) ignores NULLs. SUM and AVG only work with numeric columns. MAX and MIN work with text, dates and numbers. Use DISTINCT inside COUNT to count unique values.

programs.title: JOIN — Equi-join between two tables
programs.description: Write queries to join EMPLOYEE and DEPARTMENT tables based on a common column.
programs.code:
-- Table: EMPLOYEE (EmpNo, Name, DeptNo, Salary)
-- Table: DEPARTMENT (DeptNo, DeptName, Location)

-- Equi-Join: Display employee name with department name
SELECT E.Name, D.DeptName
FROM EMPLOYEE E, DEPARTMENT D
WHERE E.DeptNo = D.DeptNo;

-- Same using JOIN keyword
SELECT E.Name, D.DeptName, D.Location
FROM EMPLOYEE E
JOIN DEPARTMENT D ON E.DeptNo = D.DeptNo;

-- Join with additional condition
SELECT E.Name, E.Salary, D.DeptName
FROM EMPLOYEE E, DEPARTMENT D
WHERE E.DeptNo = D.DeptNo AND E.Salary > 50000;

-- Natural Join (automatically matches common column names)
SELECT Name, DeptName
FROM EMPLOYEE NATURAL JOIN DEPARTMENT;
programs.output:
-- Equi-join matches rows where join columns are equal
-- Use table aliases (E, D) for clarity
-- Natural join automatically uses common column names
-- The common column appears only once in natural join
programs.tips: Always use table aliases (E, D) when joining tables. The join condition uses the common column. In Cartesian product (without WHERE), every row of table 1 is paired with every row of table 2. CBSE prefers the comma-separated syntax with WHERE over the JOIN keyword.

programs.title: UPDATE and DELETE queries
programs.description: Write queries to update and delete records from the EMPLOYEE table.
programs.output:
-- UPDATE modifies existing data in rows
-- DELETE removes entire rows
-- Always use WHERE to target specific rows
-- Without WHERE, UPDATE/DELETE affects ALL rows
programs.tips: Always use WHERE clause with UPDATE and DELETE to avoid affecting all rows. UPDATE uses SET to specify new values. DELETE FROM removes rows, not columns. To remove a table entirely, use DROP TABLE (not DELETE).

programs.title: SQL Output Prediction — Tricky queries
programs.description: Predict the output of queries involving NULL values and string functions.
programs.output:
NULL + anything = NULL
NULL cannot be compared using = or !=
Use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL
UPPER/LOWER change case
LENGTH counts all characters including spaces
SUBSTR(string, start, length) extracts a substring
programs.tips: NULL is not zero or empty string — it means unknown/missing. Any operation with NULL gives NULL. Always use IS NULL, never = NULL. Know the common string functions: UPPER, LOWER, LENGTH, SUBSTR, LEFT, RIGHT, TRIM, INSTR.
